title: Deep learning in fetal, infant, and toddler neuroimaging research
authors: Jenna H. Chin, Madeleine K. Wyburd, Vladislav Ayzenberg, Laurie Bayet, Berkin Bilgic, Emily M. Chen, Yuting Chen, Áine Dineen, Shohei Fujita, Janelle Liu, Yohan Jun, M. Catalina Camacho, Lilla Zöllei
institution: Washington University School of Medicine; Martinos Center for Biomedical Imaging; Cedars-Sinai Medical Center
paradigm: Review of deep learning applications in FIT neuroimaging: structural image analysis, data acquisition enhancement, cognitive modeling, automated video tagging
statistical_approach: Systematic review of deep learning and machine learning methods applied to fetal, infant, and toddler neuroimaging
abstract: This review provides a concise overview of deep learning concepts before reviewing applications in fetal, infant, and toddler (FIT) neuroimaging, including structural image analysis, enhancement of data acquisition, modeling of cognitive and perceptual processes, and automated video tagging. The paper addresses challenges in medical image analysis including high clinical stakes, limited availability of labeled data, and substantial variability in imaging protocols. These challenges are especially pronounced in FIT neuroimaging where datasets are scarce and subject to large amounts of anatomical variability. The review concludes with discussion of best practices for data curation, ongoing challenges, and opportunities for future research.

title: Early psychosocial deprivation alters the refinement of neural dynamics across adolescence
authors: Marta Andujar, Lucrezia Liuzzi, Daniel S. Pine, Charles A. Nelson, Charles H. Zeanah, Nathan A. Fox, Bruno B. Averbeck
institution: National Institute of Mental Health; Boston Children's Hospital; Harvard Medical School; Tulane University; University of Maryland
journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences USA
age_range: Adolescents (longitudinal from childhood); Bucharest Early Intervention Project
paradigm: Flanker task with linear dynamical systems modeling of EEG; longitudinal cohort with early institutional deprivation vs. foster care vs. never-institutionalized controls
statistical_approach: Linear dynamical systems modeling; eigenvalue magnitude analysis reflecting temporal persistence of neural dynamics; trial-by-trial EEG residuals
abstract: Adolescence involves major behavioral and neural changes, with development of recurrent computations supporting cognitive control. This study examined how early adversity affects neural developmental trajectories by analyzing electroencephalography (EEG) activity during a Flanker task in participants from the Bucharest Early Intervention Project. Neural dynamics were modeled as a linear dynamical system. Eigenvalue magnitude—reflecting the temporal persistence of neural dynamics—decreases with age in all groups, consistent with maturation of recurrent computations. However, early psychological deprivation alters this developmental trajectory. These findings align with theoretical predictions linking excitatory synaptic pruning to changes in neural dynamics and highlight the lasting influence of early experience on adolescent network refinement.

title: A decade of discovery: Leveraging genomic data in the ABCD study to illuminate adolescent neurodevelopment
authors: Leanna M. Hernandez, Robert Loughnan, William Cheng, Le Zhang, Chun Chieh Fan, Arpana Agrawal, ABCD Genetics Workgroup
institution: University of California San Francisco; multiple ABCD consortium institutions
paradigm: Genome-wide association studies, polygenic score analyses, gene-environment interactions using ABCD Study dataset
statistical_approach: GWAS; admixture estimation; relatedness analysis; polygenic scores; gene x environment modeling
abstract: The Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study provides an unprecedented resource for developmental genomics, offering genetic data for more than 11,000 youth, as well as extensive longitudinal behavioral, neuroimaging, environmental, and real-world digital phenotyping measures. This review summarizes the scope and utility of ABCD genetics data and provides a comprehensive review of ABCD genetics publications over the past decade. Key findings include that SNP-based effects are modest and interact with the environment to shape outcomes, and that association studies show polygenic liability has broad cross-trait effects. The review also discusses key challenges including the need for robust analytic strategies to maximize use of admixed and cross-ancestry genetic data, application of advanced statistical methods to model gene x environment interactions, and the use of real-world digital phenotyping data.

title: The longitudinal development of intrinsic timescales in infancy and their relation to alpha brain rhythm
authors: Anna Truzzi, Josué Rico-Picó, Maria Rosario Rueda, Rhodri Cusack
institution: University of Granada; Basque Center on Cognition, Brain and Language (BCBL)
paradigm: Resting-state EEG during sleep; longitudinal developmental study (exploratory n=45; validation n=45)
statistical_approach: Autocorrelation analysis for intrinsic timescales; lagged coherence; spectral analysis; correlation with alpha properties
abstract: Adult brain regions differ in the intrinsic timescales (INT) over which they integrate information, with this spatial organization emerging gradually. Previous fMRI studies showed that infants' brain activity shows overall longer INT and different spatial structure compared to adults, though fMRI is sensitive to hemodynamic confounds and arousal state. This longitudinal EEG study investigated how intrinsic timescales develop in infancy using a larger sample from 6 to 16 months old (exploratory cohort, n=45; validation cohort, n=45) and adults (n=10). Results showed that infant intrinsic timescales shortened from 6 to 16 months but remained longer than those of adults at all ages. Intrinsic timescales correlated with alpha lagged coherence, a metric of self-predictability, and to a lesser extent with alpha peak frequency, suggesting that alpha oscillatory activity may contribute to the emergence of intrinsic timescales during development.

title: Caregiver unpredictability and the development of biobehavioral regulation during early childhood
authors: Elisa Ugarte, Paul D. Hastings
institution: University of California, Davis; Human Development Graduate Group
paradigm: Day/Night inhibitory control task with RSA measurement; observational caregiver unpredictability coding; longitudinal follow-up
statistical_approach: Longitudinal mixed models; RSA suppression analysis; behavioral observation coding of caregiver affective and behavioral unpredictability
abstract: Experiences of unpredictability can create significant disruptions for children's psychosocial development. This pre-registered longitudinal study developed an observational measure of caregiver affective and behavioral unpredictability in early childhood and explored its implications for preschoolers' biobehavioral self-regulation. In a sample of 98 predominantly White 4-year-old children and their mothers, greater caregiver affective and behavioral unpredictability was associated concurrently with greater respiratory sinus arrhythmia (RSA) suppression during the Day/Night task. Greater caregiver sensory signal unpredictability at 4 years predicted children's greater RSA suppression during the Day/Night task 2 years later at age 6. The research extends prior work on caregiver unpredictability with infants and provides initial evidence that unpredictable caregiver signals appear to shape early, non-volitional processes that regulate arousal in novel situations.

title: Adolescent-Specific Adaptations to Caregiving Unpredictability: A Longitudinal Examination of Cognitive Flexibility
authors: Erica Niemiec, Nim Tottenham
institution: Northeastern University; Columbia University
paradigm: NIH Toolbox Cognition Battery; executive function testing across two visits; longitudinal design uncoupling early vs. adolescent unpredictability effects
statistical_approach: Longitudinal mixed models; independent samples design separating early life and adolescent unpredictability effects on executive function
abstract: Increasing evidence for enhanced cognitive flexibility following early-life caregiving unpredictability indicates that developmental adaptations can occur during early sensitive periods. This study tested whether adaptations can also occur related to unpredictability in adolescence. Using a longitudinal design that uncouples these developmental periods in a sample enriched for early caregiving unpredictability (N=197, mean age=9.53 years at Visit 1), cognitive flexibility was examined across two visits, along with response inhibition and attentional control. Results demonstrated that unpredictable caregiving in adolescence was associated with improvements in cognitive flexibility independently from early unpredictability, suggesting that developmental adaptations are not limited to early life but may also occur in adolescence.

title: Atypical Resting-State and Task-Evoked EEG Signatures in Children with Developmental Language Disorder
authors: Aimin Liang, Zhijun Cui, Yang Shi, Chunyan Qu, Zhuang Wei, Hanxiao Wang, Xu Zhang, Xiaolin Ning, Xin Ni, Jiancheng Fang
institution: Shenzhen University School of Medicine; Beihang University
age_range: 5-10 years (children with DLD n=15 vs. typically developing n=21)
paradigm: Resting-state EEG, semantic matching task, auditory oddball task; DLD vs. typically developing children comparison
statistical_approach: Spectral connectivity analysis; microstate analysis; ERP analysis (P1, N2, difference waves); event-related desynchronization
abstract: Developmental Language Disorder (DLD) is associated with abnormalities in both intrinsic resting-state brain networks and task-evoked neural responses. This study examined multi-level EEG markers in 21 typically developing children and 15 children with DLD across resting-state, a semantic matching task, and an auditory oddball task. Resting-state analyses revealed frequency-specific connectivity imbalances, reduced stability of intrinsic microstate dynamics, and atypical transitions between microstates in the DLD group. During the semantic matching task, DLD children showed weaker occipital P1 and N2 responses (100–300 ms) and lacked the right fronto-central difference wave (500–700 ms) observed in TD children. These findings support a translational neurophysiological signature for DLD, providing a foundation for future biomarker development and targeted intervention strategies.

title: Neonatal neuroplasticity and metaplasticity: bridging neuroscience to clinical practice
authors: Emir Sahinoglu, Eunice Lo, Ahmed El Shahed, Mehmet N. Cizmeci
institution: Boston Children's Hospital; Harvard Medical School
age_range: Neonates (0-28 days); first 1000 days (conception through age 2)
paradigm: Mechanistic review of neuroplasticity and metaplasticity in neonatal brain development and clinical practice
statistical_approach: Theoretical review; synthesis of neurobiological mechanisms of synaptic plasticity, LTP/LTD, activity-dependent refinement, and metaplasticity
abstract: Neuroplasticity is the brain's adaptive ability to restructure and reorganize itself, which is maximal during the 'first 1000 days' (conception through two years of life), presenting both unique opportunities and vulnerabilities for intervention. This review focuses on the biological basis of neuroplasticity and metaplasticity in the neonatal brain, as well as their role in neurodevelopment. The article examines how the 'dynamic neural exposome' and 'toxic stressor interplay' operate during the critical neonatal period. Concepts discussed include synaptic plasticity, long-term potentiation and depression, activity-dependent refinement, and metaplasticity as a regulatory mechanism for learning and memory. The review strengthens fetal-neonatal neurology principles and practice to promote life-course brain health, with implications for clinical decision-making regarding intervention timing and choice.

title: Molecular cartography of the human Down syndrome and trisomic mouse brain
authors: Min Yi Feng, Wuxinhao Cao, Gen Li, Bharti Kukreja, Nareh Tahmasian, Bianca Rusu, Ji-Young Youn, Brian T. Kalish
institution: SickKids Research Institute, Toronto; University of Toronto
age_range: Mid-gestational prenatal human cortex; Ts65Dn mouse model
paradigm: Cross-species molecular profiling of Down syndrome: single-nucleus sequencing, spatial transcriptomics, and proteomics on human fetal cortex and trisomic mouse model
statistical_approach: Cell type-specific transcriptomic analysis; spatial gene expression mapping; comparative genomics; profiling of >240,000 cells
abstract: Down syndrome (Trisomy 21) is one of the most common genetic causes of intellectual disability, resulting in both abnormal neurodevelopment and accelerated neurodegeneration. This study performed single-nucleus sequencing, spatial transcriptomics, and proteomics on mid-gestational prenatal human cortex to gain molecular insight into the prenatal neurobiology of Down syndrome. The researchers captured altered expression dynamics of lineage commitment genes and de-repression of transposable elements in DS neural progenitor cells, suggesting changes to the fate and functionality of neuronal and glial cells. Highly multiplexed RNA in situ spatial transcriptomics was also performed on the Ts65Dn trisomic mouse model to study the cellular landscape during early life and aging, profiling over 240,000 cells and identifying trisomy-associated gene expression patterns in the molecular control of neurogenesis and gliogenesis.

title: Increased dendritic inhibition of dentate gyrus granule cells in a mouse model of Down syndrome
authors: Jan M. Schulz
institution: Department of Neurosurgery, University of Freiburg; University of Basel; Roche Innovation Center Basel
paradigm: Patch-clamp electrophysiology and optogenetic stimulation of CCK interneurons in hippocampal dentate gyrus neurons
statistical_approach: Electrophysiological recordings; IPSC amplitude measurements; optogenetic manipulation; pharmacological dissection of GABAAR subtypes
abstract: Studies have demonstrated enhanced GABAergic synaptic transmission in the hippocampus of Ts65Dn mice, a genetic model of Down syndrome. This research examined dendritic inhibition of dentate gyrus granule cells, using patch-clamp electrophysiology and optogenetic stimulation to explore mechanisms underlying cognitive deficits in Down syndrome. Activation of cholecystokinin (CCK) positive interneurons resulted in increased amplitude inhibitory postsynaptic currents (IPSCs) in the dentate gyrus. Dendritic inhibition was mediated via nonlinear alpha5-subunit containing GABAARs with kinetics and voltage dependence closely matching NMDARs, potentially affecting learning and memory processes dependent on NMDAR signaling.

title: Infant sleep EEG features at 4 months as biomarkers of neurodevelopment at 18 months
authors: Soraia Ventura, Sean R. Mathieson, John M. O'Toole, Vicki Livingstone, Deirdre M. Murray, Geraldine B. Boylan
institution: INFANT Research Centre, University College Cork, Ireland
paradigm: Longitudinal predictive study; sleep EEG features at 4 months as biomarkers of Griffiths III neurodevelopmental outcomes at 18 months
statistical_approach: Correlation analysis between sleep EEG macrostructure, spindle features, quantitative EEG and Griffiths III developmental quotients
abstract: Sleep participates in synaptic homeostasis and memory consolidation, and infant sleep parameters have been shown to correlate with later aspects of early childhood cognition. Typically developing, term-born infants underwent diurnal sleep-EEG at 4 months and Griffiths III developmental assessment at 18 months. EEG analysis included sleep macrostructure, sleep spindle features, and quantitative EEG features (interhemispheric connectivity and spectral power). Sleep latency was positively associated with the Griffiths III Foundations of Learning subscale and N3 sleep duration correlated with the Personal-Social-Emotional subscale. Sleep spindle synchrony was negatively associated with Eye and Hand Coordination, Personal-Social-Emotional, Gross Motor, and General Development quotients. Sleep spindle duration and synchrony may help predict neurodevelopmental outcomes.

title: Mother-Infant Dyadic Neural Synchrony Measured Using EEG Hyperscanning and Validated Using Behavioral Measures
authors: Mary Lauren Neel, Arnaud Jeanvoine, Caitlin P. Kjeldsen, Nathalie L. Maitre
institution: Emory University School of Medicine; Children's Healthcare of Atlanta
paradigm: EEG hyperscanning of mother-infant dyads during scaffolded sensorimotor interaction (sequential layering of visual and tactile modalities)
statistical_approach: Circular correlation coefficient (CCorr) for dyadic neural synchrony; correlation with WECS behavioral synchrony scores
abstract: Greater parent-infant synchrony is associated with improved child outcomes, but behavioral measures of synchrony are still developing in young infants. This study examined parent-infant neural synchrony using EEG hyperscanning in mother-infant dyads at 2-4 months of age with time-locked dual EEG recordings during a scaffolded interaction where the mother sequentially layered sensory modalities. Neural synchrony was measured using the circular correlation coefficient (CCorr) and infant behavioral synchrony was measured using the Welch Emotional Connection Screen (WECS). Results from n=47 dyads showed that dyadic CCorr increased across the interaction as the mother added tactile stimulation to visual stimulation. Infants with higher WECS scores showed greater increases in CCorr, supporting the construct validity of EEG hyperscanning for quantifying mother-infant dyadic neural synchrony.

title: Resting state periodic and aperiodic brain oscillations from birth to preschool years: Aperiodic maturity predicts developmental course
authors: Heather L. Green, J. Christopher Edgar, Kylie Mol, Marybeth McNamee, Laura Prosser, Mina Kim, Emily S. Kuschner, Gregory A. Miller, Yuhan Chen
institution: Lurie Family Foundations MEG Imaging Center, Children's Hospital of Philadelphia; University of Pennsylvania; UCLA
age_range: 2-68 months (107 typically developing children; birth to preschool)
paradigm: Longitudinal developmental MEG study of periodic and aperiodic resting-state neural activity; Dark Room eyes-open task vs. Video-On condition
statistical_approach: MEG source imaging; spectral power analysis; developmental trajectory modeling; behavioral correlation with adaptive behavior scales
abstract: Understanding resting-state brain activity across early development is essential for establishing maturation trajectories and improving clinical assessment opportunities. This study used MEG with source imaging and an adapted Dark Room eyes-open task to assess oscillatory and non-oscillatory resting-state activity in 107 typically developing children aged 2 to 68 months. Aperiodic measures decreased nonlinearly as a function of age, with regional differences across brain areas. Analyses in brain source space revealed regional differences in aperiodic activity previously undetectable in scalp-space analyses. Critically, more mature aperiodic values predicted better adaptive behaviors and daily living skills, pointing to potential brain mechanisms supporting behavioral development across early childhood.

title: On human synchrony: A systematic review of the origins of physiological and neural synchrony in parent-infant dyads (0-12 months)
authors: Laura Lavezzo, Kinkini Bhadra, Leonardo Ceravolo, Petra Hüppi, Enzo Pasquale Scilingo, Didier Grandjean, Julie Péron, Mimma Nardelli, Manuela Filippa
institution: University of Geneva; University of Pisa
age_range: 0-12 months (parent-infant dyads; systematic review of 42 studies)
paradigm: Systematic review of 42 studies on physiological and neural synchrony in parent-infant dyads (27 peripheral physiological, 15 central neural EEG/fNIRS)
statistical_approach: Systematic review methodology; narrative synthesis of synchrony measures (ECG, respiration, EEG, fNIRS) across diverse paradigms and populations
abstract: This systematic review examined the origins and nature of physiological and neural synchrony in parent-infant dyads during the first 12 months of life. Through a review of 42 studies, the authors found that parent-infant synchrony is a dynamic, context-dependent process that is strongest during embodied, multisensory, affectively charged interactions. The review covered 27 studies using peripheral physiological measures (ECG, respiration) and 15 studies with central neural measures (EEG, fNIRS). Findings indicate that synchrony emerges as a bidirectional dyadic process across the first year of life and is associated with infant development outcomes, with important methodological considerations for measuring synchrony across different modalities and interaction contexts.

title: Developmental human brain connectome from fetal stage to early childhood
authors: Wentao Wu, Hao Huang, Tianjia Zhu, Shufang Tan, Minhui Ouyang
institution: University of Pennsylvania; Shenzhen Institutes of Advanced Technology
paradigm: Review of structural and functional human brain connectome development from fetal stage through early childhood
statistical_approach: Review of connectome analysis methods; network integration/segregation metrics; hierarchical maturation modeling; connectivity-disorder associations
abstract: The human brain undergoes rapid maturation from the fetal stage through early childhood with shifts from network segregation to integration, with the connectome reshaped hierarchically from primary to higher-order regions. This review documents structural and functional brain connectome development across the fetal to early childhood period, examining how microstructural and physiological changes drive connectome development. The paper addresses canonical developmental trajectories and identifies altered connectivity patterns associated with autism and schizophrenia, providing a framework for understanding how early connectome organization shapes later cognitive outcomes and neurodevelopmental disorder risk.

title: Evaluating oxidative stress marker F2alpha-Isoprostanes relations to early life adversity and as a predictor of neurodevelopment in infants and toddlers
authors: Kameelah Gateau, Ramón Angel Durazo-Arvizu, Pat Levitt
institution: Keck School of Medicine, University of Southern California; The Saban Research Institute, Children's Hospital Los Angeles
age_range: Infants and toddlers (6 months primary assessment; trajectory to 12 months)
paradigm: Longitudinal biomarker study; F2alpha-Isoprostanes as oxidative stress marker predicting neurodevelopmental outcomes stratified by maternal ACEs
statistical_approach: Trajectory analysis by ACE group; correlation with Bayley-4 language and motor scores; biomarker validation
abstract: Elevated F2alpha-Isoprostanes (F2alpha-IsoPs) have been proposed as gold standard oxidative stress markers. This study evaluated F2alpha-IsoPs in relation to early life adversity and neurodevelopmental outcomes in infants and toddlers. Elevated infant F2alpha-IsoPs at 6 months correlated with lower language scores (r=-0.15) and lower motor scores (r=-0.16) on the Bayley-4 developmental assessment. Different F2alpha-IsoPs trajectories were identified based on maternal adverse childhood experiences (0, 1-2, or 3+ ACEs), with higher maternal ACE burden associated with elevated infant oxidative stress. These findings suggest a biological pathway through which early life adversity and environmental stress may impact neurodevelopment in the first year of life.

title: Neural reorganization of dialect perception within a critical two-month window in infancy: A longitudinal fNIRS study
authors: Nikola Paillereau, Sára Mádrová, Torsten Wüstenberg
paradigm: Longitudinal fNIRS study; familiar (Standard Czech) vs. unfamiliar dialect (Silesian Czech) processing at 4 and 6 months
statistical_approach: Longitudinal within-subject design; right prefrontal cortex activation analysis; familiarity vs. novelty preference comparison across ages
abstract: This longitudinal fNIRS study examined how Czech-learning infants (N=37) process differences between Standard Czech (familiar) and Silesian dialect (unfamiliar) between 4 and 6 months of age. At 4 months, infants showed stronger right prefrontal cortex responses to Standard Czech, reflecting a neural familiarity preference. By 6 months, responses to the unfamiliar dialect increased significantly, reflecting neural reorganization of language-relevant auditory processing. The study demonstrates critical neural reorganization of dialect perception during a two-month window in early language development, supporting the role of early acoustic exposure in shaping infant neural responses to phonological variation.

title: Resting State Electroencephalography (EEG) Reveals Atypical Oscillatory Power in Children With Developmental Coordination Disorder (DCD)
authors: Jarrad A. G. Lum, Kaila Hamilton, Ian Fuelscher, Pamela Barhoun, Frederik J. A. Deconinck, Arthur De Raeve, Talitha C. Ford, Tim Silk, Peter G. Enticott, Gayatri Kumar, Dwayne Meaney, Mugdha Mukherjee, Jessica Waugh, Christian Hyde
institution: Deakin University; Ghent University; University of Newcastle
age_range: ~9.5 years (children with DCD n=31 vs. typically developing n=52)
paradigm: Resting-state EEG in eyes-open and eyes-closed conditions; DCD vs. typically developing controls
statistical_approach: Spectral analysis of alpha and delta band power; group comparisons; correlations between EEG features and motor functioning
abstract: This study examined resting-state EEG in 31 children with developmental coordination disorder (DCD) and 52 age-matched typically developing controls (mean age 9.5 years) in both eyes-open and eyes-closed conditions. Children with DCD exhibited significantly lower alpha power and higher delta power compared to controls, indicating heightened neural excitability and suboptimal neural homeostatic regulation. Individual differences in motor functioning correlated with resting-state alpha and delta power for the DCD group but not controls. These findings suggest that atypical oscillatory power in DCD reflects disrupted neural homeostatic mechanisms that may underlie motor coordination difficulties, with potential implications for EEG biomarker development in developmental motor disorders.

title: Longitudinal habituation and novelty detection neural responses from infancy to early childhood in The Gambia and UK
authors: Anna Blasi Ribera, Borja Blanco Maniega, Samantha McCann, Ebrima Mbye, Ebou Touray, Maria Rozhko, Bosiljka Milosavljevic, Laura Katus, Mariama Saidykhan, Muhammed Ceesay, Tijan Fadera, Giulia Ghillia, Marta Perapoch Amado, Maria M. Crespo-Llado, Sophie E. Moore, Clare E. Elwell, Sarah Lloyd-Fox
institution: University College London; MRC Unit The Gambia; Queen Mary University of London
age_range: 5-60 months (Gambian n=204; UK n=61; BRIGHT project; longitudinal 5 visits)
paradigm: Longitudinal fNIRS study of habituation and novelty detection neural responses across 5 visits (5-60 months); cross-cultural comparison (The Gambia vs UK)
statistical_approach: Longitudinal growth modeling; cross-cultural mixed-effects models; within-subject response trajectories across 5 time points
abstract: This longitudinal study from the BRIGHT (Brain Imaging for Global health) project examined habituation and novelty detection (HaND) neural responses in Gambian (N=204) and UK (N=61) infants from 5 to 60 months. Gambian infants showed persistent habituation responses across all visits but novelty detection emerged only at 18-24 months. UK infants showed habituation only from 5-12 months with early novelty detection from 5-12 months. The study reveals significant cultural and developmental differences in early neural responses to novelty, suggesting environmental factors shape neural development. The cross-cultural longitudinal design provides unique insights into how early experiences across diverse environments influence the developmental trajectory of perceptual processing.

title: Growth in early infancy drives optimal brain functional connectivity which predicts cognitive flexibility in later childhood
authors: Chiara Bulgarelli, Anna Blasi, Samantha McCann, Bosiljka Milosavljevic, Giulia Ghillia, Ebrima Mbye, Ebou Touray, Tijan Fadera, Lena Acolatse, Sophie E Moore, Sarah Lloyd-Fox, Clare E Elwell, Adam T Eggebrecht
institution: University College London; Washington University School of Medicine; MRC Unit The Gambia
paradigm: Resting-state fNIRS; longitudinal growth modeling; cognitive flexibility follow-up at 3-5 years
statistical_approach: Longitudinal growth modeling; functional connectivity analysis; predictive modeling of preschool outcomes
abstract: The study examined how functional brain network organization reflects key neurodevelopmental processes, and how early adversity such as undernutrition affects neurodevelopment through disrupted functional connectivity. Using fNIRS in a rural Gambian cohort, the researchers assessed longitudinally the impact of early growth trajectories on developmental functional connectivity from age 5-24 months, and investigated how these early trajectories relate to later childhood cognitive flexibility at 3-5 years. They found that early physical growth before the fifth month of life drove optimal developmental trajectories of functional connectivity that in turn predicted cognitive flexibility at pre-school age.

title: Canonical neurodevelopmental trajectories of structural and functional manifolds
authors: Alicja Monaghan, Richard A.I. Bethlehem, Danyal Akarca, Daniel Margulies, Duncan E. Astle, CALM Team
institution: MRC Cognition and Brain Sciences Unit, University of Cambridge
paradigm: Structural and functional connectivity gradient analysis; manifold learning; developmental trajectories
statistical_approach: Dimensionality reduction (manifold learning); canonical trajectories; neurotypical vs neurodivergent group comparisons
abstract: This study provides insights into the neurodevelopmental trajectories of structural and functional connectivity gradients in the human brain and their potential associations with behaviour and psychopathology. The paper presents evidence of alterations in and stability of the principal organizational gradients of structure and function across development and contrasts them between neurotypical and neurodivergent individuals from the CALM dataset.

title: The association between infant EEG aperiodic exponent and the trajectory of restricted and repetitive behaviors for toddlers with and without autism
authors: Haerin Chung, Alex Job Said, Helen Tager-Flusberg, Charles A. Nelson, Carol L. Wilkinson
institution: Boston Children's Hospital; Harvard Medical School; Boston University
paradigm: Resting-state EEG; aperiodic exponent; longitudinal RRB trajectories
statistical_approach: Mixed-effects models; aperiodic (FOOOF/specparam) exponent analysis; E/I balance proxy
abstract: This study evaluated the relationship between aperiodic exponent (as a proxy of E/I balance) and changes in restricted and repetitive behaviors (RRBs) over time in infants with and without elevated likelihood of autism. Resting-state EEG data were collected from 12-to-14-month-old infants and aperiodic exponent was calculated. Parent-reported RRBs were obtained using the Repetitive Behavior Scale-Revised questionnaire to measure the severity and change in RRBs from 12-to-36 months. Lower aperiodic exponent was associated with elevated RRBs reported over time.

title: Characterization of aperiodic and theta activity in preterm infants using EEG: Insights into cerebral maturation and inter-individual variability
authors: Aline Gonzalez-Carpinteiro, Hala Nasser, Amandine Pedoux, Laurie Devisscher, et al.
institution: Universite Paris Cite, INSERM, NeuroDiderot; Robert-Debre Hospital, APHP, Paris
paradigm: Resting-state EEG; spectral parameterization; aperiodic and periodic theta in preterms vs full-terms
statistical_approach: Spectral parameterization (FOOOF); mixed-effects models; brain microstructure correlates
abstract: Preterm birth interferes with the maturation of brain networks and functional activity. Theta oscillations are thought to play a key role in early network formation, but their maturation appears vulnerable to prematurity. Traditional EEG spectral analyses mix oscillatory and non-oscillatory activity, limiting insights. Using spectral parametrisation, this study evaluated developmental changes in aperiodic activity and periodic theta power in infants born very preterm compared to full-terms, and explored whether clinical factors and brain microstructure could explain inter-individual variability within preterms.

title: Infants' resting-state functional connectivity and event-related potentials: A multimodal approach to investigating the neural basis of infant novelty detection
authors: Dana Kanel, Santiago Morales, Kathryn Altman, John E. Richards, Anderson M. Winkler, Daniel S. Pine, Nathan A. Fox, Courtney A. Filippi
institution: National Institute of Mental Health; University of Maryland; University of Texas Rio Grande Valley
paradigm: Resting-state fMRI; auditory oddball ERP; multimodal EEG-fMRI integration
statistical_approach: Source-localized EEG; resting-state functional connectivity; ERP-fc correlation; somatomotor and attention networks
abstract: This study balanced spatial and temporal precision by combining ERP data with fMRI data in infants. Twenty-nine infants underwent resting-state fMRI and EEG during a three-stimulus auditory oddball task. Mismatch response (MMR) and P3 were computed from ERP data, and resting-state functional connectivity (rs-FC) was computed from fMRI. Source-localized EEG analyses implicated bilateral auditory cortices, posterior cingulate cortex, and superior parietal cortex in the generation of MMR and P3 responses. The MMR and P3 related to functional connectivity within the somatomotor network as well as between the somatomotor and dorsal and ventral attention networks.

title: Neural Responses to Caregivers After Early Life Threat Experiences
authors: Nicolas Murgueitio, Michelle Shipkova, Lucy A. Lurie, Micaela Rodriguez, Laura Machlin, Maresa Tate, Sneha Boda, Zoe Priddy, Cathi B. Propper, Katie A. McLaughlin, Regina M. Sullivan, Margaret A. Sheridan
institution: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill; Harvard University; NYU Grossman School of Medicine
paradigm: Neural responses to caregiver cues; early life threat adversity; ERP
statistical_approach: ERP analysis; multilevel models; adversity x caregiver cue interaction
abstract: Evidence from rodent studies highlights the mother as a safety cue that regulates fear and biology. When infant rats are exposed to rough maternal care (threat), their brains show atypical patterns of activity in response to maternal cues. This study tested whether childhood adversity characterized by threat (physical abuse, domestic violence) influences neural responses to caregiver cues in children. Children who had experienced early life threat showed differential neural responses to caregiver cues compared to controls, extending rodent findings of threat-induced disruption of maternal safety signaling to human development.

title: Prenatal substance exposure and infant neurodevelopment: a review of magnetic resonance imaging studies
authors: Leela Shah, Christy D. Yoon, Alessandra M. LaJeunesse, Lilly G. Schirmer, Emma W. Rapallini, Elizabeth M. Planalp, Douglas C. Dean
institution: University of Wisconsin-Madison
paradigm: Systematic review; prenatal substance exposure neuroimaging
statistical_approach: Systematic review; narrative synthesis of 25 peer-reviewed MRI studies
abstract: This review examines the impact of prenatal substance use on fetal brain development using MRI as a critical non-invasive tool, summarizing findings from 25 peer-reviewed studies that leverage structural, functional, and diffusion MRI to examine the effects of prenatal exposure to alcohol, opioids, methamphetamines, cocaine, nicotine, or cannabis. Particular attention was given to studies that paired infant MRI data with developmental outcomes. Existing research has implicated cortical and sub-cortical gray and white matter regions across substance exposures, with associations between MRI findings and developmental outcomes in infancy.

title: Multimodal analysis of neural signals related to source memory encoding in young children
authors: Yiyi Lei, John E. Richards, Fengji Geng, Tracy Riggins
institution: University of Maryland; University of South Carolina
paradigm: fMRI-informed EEG source localization; source memory encoding; P2 and late slow wave
statistical_approach: fMRI-constrained EEG source localization; medial temporal lobe parcellation; memory performance prediction
abstract: This study identified fMRI-informed cortical sources of two EEG signals during memory encoding — the P2 and the late slow wave (LSW) — that predicted subsequent source memory performance in children aged 4 to 8 years. Both P2 and LSW were source localized to cortical areas of the medial temporal lobe (MTL), reflecting MTL's role in both early-stage information processing and late-stage integration of memory. The P2 effect was localized to all six tested MTL subregions bilaterally, whereas the LSW effect was localized to parahippocampal cortex and entorhinal cortex.

title: EEG time-frequency dynamics of early cognitive control development
authors: Santiago Morales, George A. Buzzell
institution: University of Maryland; Florida International University
paradigm: Review of EEG time-frequency analysis of cognitive control; midfrontal theta and delta
statistical_approach: Narrative review; synthesis of midfrontal theta/delta oscillation studies across development
abstract: This review examines when and how the neural dynamics of cognitive control emerge and develop, focusing on EEG time-frequency measures used to study cognitive control in infants and children. The authors argue that time-frequency analyses capture two distinct components of cognitive control: detection that control is needed, and instantiation of control. Starting in infancy and increasing through adolescence, midfrontal theta and delta oscillations are involved in control detection. Theta band connectivity between midfrontal and lateral-frontal cortices is present from early childhood for control instantiation. Individual differences in midfrontal theta link early adversity to future psychopathology.

title: Social Determinants of Health, the developing brain, and risk and resilience for psychopathology
authors: Rachel E. Lean, Anna Constantino-Pettit, Lisa S. Gorham, Max P. Herzberg, Berenice Anaya, Cynthia E. Rogers, Joan L. Luby
institution: Washington University in St. Louis
paradigm: Review of social determinants of health and developing brain; risk and resilience for psychopathology
statistical_approach: Narrative review; synthesis of neuroimaging, physiological, and behavioral evidence
abstract: Social Determinants of Health (SDoH) are defined as non-biological individual and structural factors shaping the socio-environmental conditions of everyday life, and SDoH are major drivers of risk for psychiatric disorders across development. This review synthesizes evidence linking SDoH including poverty, adversity, and social disadvantage to developing brain structure and function, as well as individual factors that confer risk and resilience for psychopathology.

title: The neural correlates of response inhibition across the transition from infancy to toddlerhood: An fNIRS study
authors: Abigail Fiske, Liam Collins-Jones, Carina de Klerk, Katie Y.K. Lui, Alexandra Hendry, Isobel Greenhalgh, Anna Hall, Henrik Dvergsdal, Gaia Scerif, Karla Holmboe
institution: Lancaster University; University of Cambridge; University of Essex; University of Oxford
paradigm: Response inhibition task; longitudinal fNIRS from 10 to 16 months
statistical_approach: General linear model; fNIRS channel analysis; laterality analysis; prefrontal-parietal network
abstract: Using fNIRS and an age-appropriate response inhibition task, this pre-registered study investigated brain regions associated with response inhibition in 16-month-old toddlers, following the same cohort from a previous study at 10 months. While 10-month-old infants recruited right-lateralised regions of the PFC and parietal cortex when inhibition was required, by 16 months toddlers recruited the left superior parietal gyrus, the right inferior frontal gyrus, and bilateral regions of the dorsolateral PFC and orbital frontal cortex, suggesting a critical period for inhibitory control development in the first 16 months of life.

title: Exploring neurodevelopment through oscillatory and aperiodic EEG activity: methodological and clinical considerations
authors: Stefania Petri, Helene Vitali, Claudio Campus, Martina Riberto, Monica Gori
institution: Italian Institute of Technology; University of Genoa
paradigm: Review of oscillatory and aperiodic EEG methodology in neurodevelopment; clinical applications
statistical_approach: Narrative review; spectral parameterization methods; clinical and developmental applications
abstract: Understanding the neural dynamics that characterize early brain development is a central goal in neuroscience. This review is structured into three sections: (i) methodological considerations for analyzing oscillatory and aperiodic components of EEG spectrum; (ii) developmental changes in EEG spectral features during early childhood; (iii) alterations in spectral activity in children with developmental disorders. EEG spectral analysis reveals two key components: oscillatory activity associated with cognitive processes and aperiodic activity reflecting scale-free neural processes.

title: Theta activity as a marker of cognitive development in infancy: A longitudinal study across the first two years of life
authors: Alicja Brzozowska, Johanna Ruess, Regina Ori Stoeckl, Martina Arioli, Stefanie Hoehl
institution: University of Vienna
paradigm: Longitudinal EEG; frontocentral theta; novelty-related theta modulation; cognitive outcomes at 24 months
statistical_approach: Mixed-effects models; longitudinal stability of theta indices; predictive modeling of cognitive and language outcomes
abstract: This study related three different indices of frontocentral theta activity (absolute and relative power, and an index of theta modulation by novel content) at 6 and 12 months to cognitive development level, language skills, and visual attention at 24 months. Theta power increased over the course of novel information encoding at 6 and 12 months. Both absolute and relative theta power showed a large degree of stability in individual differences from 6 to 12 months. Absolute theta power at 6 and 12 months was a positive predictor of general cognitive level at 24 months.

title: Resting state EEG classifies developmental status in three-year-old children
authors: Dhanya Parameshwaran, Supriya Bhavnani, Debarati Mukherjee, Kamal Kant Sharma, Jennifer Jane Newson, Narayan P. Subramaniyam, Gauri Divan, Vikram Patel, Tara C. Thiagarajan
institution: Sapien Labs; Sangath, India; Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health
paradigm: Resting-state EEG; spectral and time-domain features; BSID-III developmental classification
statistical_approach: Machine learning classification; EEG spectral features; BSID-III outcomes; low-resource rural India cohort
abstract: This study examined how well a combination of spectral and time-domain features derived from resting-state EEG could identify children whose BSID-III scores fell above and below one standard deviation of the mean across cognitive, expressive language, receptive language, gross motor, and fine motor domains. The research was carried out in three-year-old children from low resource households in rural India, a setting which remains largely under-represented in global neurophysiological research.

title: Functional specialization for language processing in inferior frontal regions during early childhood: evidence from functional near-infrared spectroscopy individual functional channels of interest approach
authors: Haolun Luo, Tao Yu, Qun Li, Li Sheng
institution: Hong Kong Polytechnic University; Shanghai Jiao Tong University; West China Hospital of Sichuan University
paradigm: Language processing task; individual functional channel of interest (fCOI) approach; toddlers
statistical_approach: Individual fCOI approach; language-selective vs cognitive control region dissociation; mixed-effects models
abstract: This study addressed whether specialized language regions exist from early life or emerge gradually through development. Researchers validated an individual functional channel of interest (fCOI) approach for dissociating language and cognitive control regions in adults and applied this method to toddlers using fNIRS. Participants included 20 adults (ages 18-26) and 22 toddlers (ages 2-4) who completed language processing and cognitive control tasks. The study demonstrated early functional specialization in the language-selective region of left inferior frontal gyrus, which showed selective responses to linguistic content in toddlers.

title: Age-related patterns of resting EEG power in infancy: Associations with prenatal socioeconomic disadvantage
authors: Aislinn Sandre, Sonya V. Troller-Renfree, Melina Amarante, Amanda M. Dettmer, Jerrold S. Meyer, Kimberly G. Noble
institution: Columbia University; University of Massachusetts Amherst
paradigm: Resting-state EEG power; longitudinal; prenatal socioeconomic disadvantage
statistical_approach: Mixed-effects models; EEG band power trajectories; prenatal income-to-needs moderation
abstract: This study found that resting EEG power exhibits non-linear age-related patterns during the first 18 months of life. Prenatal family income-to-needs (ITN) moderated age-related patterns of theta and beta power, with infants from families with lower ITN showing less steep decreases in theta power and less steep increases in beta power. These associations were specific to family ITN and not parental education, and held after adjusting for prenatal, postnatal, and infant demographic and health-related factors.

title: Oscillatory But Not Aperiodic Frontal Brain Activity Predicts the Development of Executive Control From Infancy to Toddlerhood
authors: Josue Rico-Pico, M. Del Carmen Garcia-de-Soria Bazan, Angela Conejero, Sebastian Moyano, Angela Hoyo, Maria de Los Angeles Ballesteros-Duperon, Karla Holmboe, M. Rosario Rueda
institution: University of Granada; University of Cambridge
paradigm: Longitudinal EEG; frontal oscillatory and aperiodic activity; inhibitory control and cognitive flexibility
statistical_approach: Longitudinal regression; alpha and theta power; aperiodic exponent; executive control prediction
abstract: Executive control (EC) emerges in the first year of life, with inhibitory control (IC) and cognitive flexibility (CF) steadily improving. This study examined whether oscillatory or aperiodic frontal EEG activity predicted EC development from infancy to toddlerhood. Alpha and theta oscillatory activity were concurrent (9 months) and longitudinal predictors of cognitive flexibility in toddlerhood, whereas the aperiodic exponent did not contribute to predicting EC, dissociating oscillatory from aperiodic contributions to early cognitive development.

title: Charting brain functional development from birth to 6 years of age
authors: Weiyan Yin, Tengfei Li, Zhengwang Wu, Sheng-Che Hung, Dan Hu, et al., UNC/UMN Baby Connectome Project Consortium
institution: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
paradigm: Resting-state fMRI; longitudinal developmental charts; 1,091 scans; canonical brain networks
statistical_approach: Harmonized longitudinal modeling; normative developmental charts of FC; neurodevelopmental milestone identification
abstract: Early childhood is crucial for brain functional development. Using advanced neuroimaging, this study aggregated 1,091 resting-state fMRI scans of typically developing children from birth to 6 years, harmonized cohort and imaging-state-related bias, and delineated developmental charts of functional connectivity within and between canonical brain networks. These charts revealed potential neurodevelopmental milestones and elucidated the complex development of brain functional integration, competition, and transition.

title: Energy-Efficient EEG-Based Autism Spectrum Disorder Detection Using a Hyperbolic Attention Neural Network
authors: Anupama J., Anshad A.S., Padmanaban K., L. Guganathan
institution: IIT Delhi; Koneru Lakshmaiah Education Foundation; SIMATS Chennai
paradigm: EEG-based ASD classification; deep learning; wearable sensor energy efficiency
statistical_approach: Hyperbolic Cross-Head Attention-Based Neural Network (HyperCrossNet); deep reversible learning; 99.92% classification accuracy
abstract: This study introduced an energy-aware, sensor-based scheme for ASD detection from EEG signals using a Hyperbolic Cross-Head Attention-Based Neural Network (HyperCrossNet). The model proposes deep reversible learning in conjunction with spatial and channel-oriented attention mechanisms. Experimental outcomes indicate 99.92% classification accuracy, 99.91% recall, and 99.90% F1-score while considerably lowering energy consumption for transmitting raw EEG data, enabling effective wearable biosensing for ASD.

title: The fractal dimension of resting state EEG increases over age in children
authors: Si Long Jenny Tou, Tom Chau
institution: University of Toronto; Holland Bloorview Kids Rehabilitation Hospital
paradigm: Resting-state EEG; Higuchi Fractal Dimension; developmental trajectory from childhood to adulthood
statistical_approach: Higuchi Fractal Dimension (HFD); MIPDB database; 83 participants; topographic analysis
abstract: Resting-state EEG represents spontaneous neural activity and is increasingly analyzed using nonlinear measures to assess brain complexity. The Higuchi Fractal Dimension (HFD) is a widely used metric for quantifying the fractal properties of EEG signals, yet its developmental trajectory remains largely unexplored. Using eyes-closed resting-state EEG from 128 channels in 83 neurotypical participants (8 to 30 years old) from the MIPDB database, a significant increase in HFD with age was observed (P = 0.001), most pronounced between childhood and adolescence, followed by stabilization in early adulthood.

title: Benchmarking functional brain network organization in childhood and its similarity to adults
authors: Ali, S.A., Demeter, D.V., Baim, A.R., Koithan, E.M., Chang, S.E., Gordon, E.M., Greene, D.J.
institution: University of California, San Diego; Washington University School of Medicine, St. Louis
paradigm: Resting-state fMRI; functional network parcellation; ABCD Study
statistical_approach: Functional network parcellation; similarity metrics vs. adult templates; matched discovery-replication cohorts; N=7,316
abstract: This study benchmarks population-level functional brain network organization in 9-10-year-old children (N=7,316 from the ABCD Study) and compares it to adult network architecture across matched discovery and replication cohorts. Functional networks in childhood showed high similarity to adult network organization, with differences concentrated in association cortex regions that continue maturing. This large-sample normative reference supports future studies linking childhood network organization to cognitive ability, behavior, and developmental disorders.

title: The development of EEG microstates from infancy into middle childhood and their associations with behavior
authors: Kelsey, C., et al.
institution: Boston Children's Hospital; Harvard Medical School; University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign
paradigm: Resting-state EEG microstates; longitudinal developmental trajectory
statistical_approach: EEG microstate analysis; mixed-effects models; age x sex interactions; behavioral associations
abstract: This study examines developmental changes in EEG microstates from infancy through 7 years (N=383 typically developing children) and associations with temperament and psychopathology. Results show complex age x sex x microstate number interactions for temporal features including occurrence, duration, coverage, and global explained variance. EEG microstates showed systematic developmental change, and specific microstate dynamics were associated with behavioral outcomes, demonstrating that EEG-derived neural dynamics reflect developmentally meaningful variation from infancy through middle childhood.

title: Spatiotemporal dynamics of EEG microstate networks over the first two years of life: A multi-cohort longitudinal study
authors: Ghosh, P., Donald, K.A., Polanczyk, G.V., Rodriguez, J.P., Shephard, E., Gabard-Durnam, L.J.
institution: Northeastern University, Center for Cognitive and Brain Health; University of Cape Town; Universidade de Sao Paulo
statistical_approach: Data-driven microstate clustering; longitudinal mixed-effects models; multi-cohort replication
abstract: Using longitudinal resting-state EEG from 854 infants across two diverse cohorts, this study identifies conserved EEG microstate network configurations during the first two years of life. Longitudinal changes included more frequent and rapid transitions between microstate classes in early infancy; sensory microstates showed consistent development across cohorts while higher-order cognitive microstates showed context-specific trends. The multi-cohort design spanning Cape Town and Sao Paulo enables cross-cultural generalization of infant EEG microstate developmental trajectories.

title: Neurodevelopmental forecasting in preterms: aEEG at 32 weeks vs. MRI at term in predicting 2-year outcomes
authors: Ancora, G., Tarocco, A., Natile, M., et al.
institution: Neonatal Intensive Care Unit, Ospedale Maggiore, Bologna, Italy
age_range: 32 weeks gestational age; outcomes at 24 months
paradigm: Amplitude-integrated EEG (aEEG); neonatal MRI; neurodevelopmental follow-up
statistical_approach: LASSO regression; ROC analysis (AUC); Bayley-III; prospective cohort design
abstract: This prospective cohort study compared the predictive accuracy of amplitude-integrated EEG (aEEG) at 32 weeks corrected age versus brain MRI at term-equivalent age in 71 infants born before 30 weeks. Burdjalov aEEG and MRI scores both significantly predicted Bayley-III outcomes. At 12 months, AUCs were 0.93 for aEEG and 0.96 for MRI; at 24 months, AUCs declined but MRI remained superior (0.92 vs. 0.78). A combined model showed highest accuracy, supporting complementary use of both modalities for early neurodevelopmental forecasting in very preterm infants.

title: Analytical pipeline optimisation in developmental fNIRS hyperscanning data: Neural coherence between 4- to 6-year old children collaborating with their mothers
authors: St. Clair, V., Contini, L., Re, R., Pinti, P., Mareschal, D.
institution: Birkbeck, University of London, Centre for Brain and Cognitive Development; Politecnico di Milano
paradigm: Hyperscanning; parent-child dyadic fNIRS; collaborative problem-solving
statistical_approach: Neural coherence analysis; physiological noise reduction; phase-scrambled pseudodyad control; best-practices pipeline optimization
abstract: This study measures neural coherence between 4- to 6-year-old children and their mothers using wearable fNIRS in a collaborative problem-solving hyperscanning paradigm, incorporating best practices in fNIRS data processing. Physiological noise artificially inflated coherence; after noise reduction, coherence remained stronger during collaborative than individual problem solving, and phase-scrambled pseudodyad analyses confirmed genuine interpersonal temporal dynamics. The optimized pipeline provides methodological guidance for developmental fNIRS hyperscanning research.

title: Altered brain state dynamics between preterm and term-born infants
authors: Damera, S.R., Basu, S., Kapse, K., Murnick, J., Andescavage, N., Limperopoulos, C., De Asis-Cruz, J.
institution: Children's National Hospital, Developing Brain Institute, Washington, DC
statistical_approach: Leading Eigenvector Dynamics Analysis (LEiDA); brain state occupancy and transition probabilities; group comparison
abstract: Applying Leading Eigenvector Analysis (LEiDA) to resting-state fMRI data from 86 term-born and 102 preterm-born infants at term-equivalent age, this study identifies four discrete brain states and examines how prematurity alters their dynamics. Term-born infants spent more time in a Transmodal State resembling the adult Default Mode Network, while preterm birth was associated with transitioning toward states dominated by sensory processing or subcortical-cortical dissociation. These findings demonstrate that prematurity alters not just static brain network organization but also the dynamics of transitions between functional states.

title: Developmental Brain Age Estimation From MRI Data: A Systematic Review of Deep Learning Approaches and Open Datasets
authors: Ull, A., et al.
institution: Asan Medical Center, University of Ulsan College of Medicine, Seoul; University Children's Hospital Zurich
paradigm: Structural/developmental MRI; brain age estimation via deep learning
statistical_approach: Systematic review of deep learning models; brain age gap analysis; open-dataset benchmarking
abstract: This systematic review provides a comprehensive overview of deep learning methodologies for developmental brain age estimation from MRI data, focusing on the fetal stage through 2 years. The review covers clinical and technical aspects of brain age estimation, catalogs open-access developmental MRI datasets, and compares model performance using standardized evaluation metrics. Brain age gap is highlighted as a quantitative biomarker for detecting atypical neurodevelopment and facilitating clinical decision-making.

title: The development of semantic integration in bilingual toddlers measured by N400
authors: Lozano, I., Duszyk-Bogorodzka, A., Ribu, I.S., Falkiewicz, N., Ogonowska, W., Dynak, A., Koder, F., Tomalski, P., Fryzowska, E., Krajewski, G., Rummelhoff, C., Varona, E.C., Krupa-Gawel, K., Laumann, L., Garmann, N.G., Haman, E.
institution: Institute of Psychology, Polish Academy of Sciences
paradigm: Picture-word priming; N400 ERP; bilingual vs. monolingual
statistical_approach: ERP analysis; N400 component; mixed-effects models; longitudinal within-subjects design
abstract: This study investigates the developmental trajectory of semantic integration in bilingual and monolingual toddlers at 18 and 24 months using a picture-word priming task while measuring N400 ERPs. Semantic integration was observed for monolingual toddlers at both time points, but not in bilinguals at either age, with no evidence of differences between bilinguals' dominant vs. non-dominant language. These findings suggest that bilingual language exposure alters the timing of semantic network development, with implications for understanding word meaning acquisition in dual-language environments.

title: Touch Modulates Gamma-Band Network Dynamics in the Infant Brain
authors: Polver, S., Addabbo, M., Turati, C., Bulf, H.
institution: Universita degli Studi di Milano-Bicocca, Department of Psychology
paradigm: Tactile stimulation (felt vs. observed touch); EEG gamma oscillations
statistical_approach: EEG spectral analysis; gamma-band power and coherence; within-infant task comparison
abstract: This study investigates the neural networks underlying naturalistic hand-to-hand touch in 8-month-old infants using EEG. Felt touch elicited widespread, highly coordinated gamma-band activity across sensory and higher-order cortical areas, supporting early integration of bodily experiences with attentional and perceptual systems. Using ecologically valid caregiver-like tactile interactions rather than artificial stimuli, the findings reveal that gamma-band cortical networks are organized to process social touch from early in postnatal life.

title: Adults with Down syndrome display altered entrainment of occipital cortical neurons
authors: Chinen, L., Busboom, M.T., Meejang, J., Kastner, O., Heinrichs-Graham, E., Wilson, T.W., Kurz, M.J.
institution: University of Nebraska Medical Center
paradigm: Steady-state visual evoked response; neural entrainment
statistical_approach: Neural entrainment analysis; comparison of oscillatory power and phase locking between groups
abstract: Adults with Down syndrome exhibit substantially weaker entrainment of occipital cortical neurons and elevated spontaneous activity during the prestimulation baseline compared with neurotypical controls. These alterations suggest impaired integrity of occipital neural populations attributable to an imbalance in local GABAergic activity and/or disruption of cholinergic pathways, affecting the dynamic range of cortical populations and contributing to perceptual and cognitive processing differences in Down syndrome.

title: Speech Auditory Brainstem Response to Predict Language Delay
authors: Wong, P.C.M., Pan, S., Lai, C.M., Chan, P.H.Y., Feng, G., Lam, H.S., Leung, T.Y., Novitskiy, N., Leung, T.F.
institution: Chinese University of Hong Kong
paradigm: Speech auditory brainstem response (speech ABR); neural speech encoding
statistical_approach: LASSO regression; ROC analysis; longitudinal cohort; neural speech encoding prediction of language outcomes
abstract: This study develops and validates predictive models of language delay using neural speech encoding data from as early as 1 month to forecast language delay before preschool. Speech auditory brainstem response (speech ABR) was recorded from 423 Chinese-learning infants between 1 and 24 months, with language outcomes collected from the same children at 7-32 months. Neural speech encoding measures predicted later language delay with high accuracy, demonstrating the potential of early ABR-based biomarkers for clinical screening and early intervention referral.

title: Infant sensory gating and a developmental cascade to autistic traits and anxiety
authors: Schwarzlose, R.F.
institution: Washington University in St. Louis School of Medicine, Department of Psychiatry
statistical_approach: N/A (perspective with mechanistic model)
abstract: This perspective proposes a developmental cascade in which impaired sensory gating during a sensitive period of neonatal neurodevelopment promotes sensory over-responsivity, autistic traits, anxiety, and other psychiatric challenges. The model links neonatal EEG-based sensory gating to downstream developmental outcomes, and highlights infant sensory responsivity as an important intermediate phenotype for research, screening, and supportive intervention in autism spectrum conditions and anxiety disorders.

title: Early-life neural correlates of behavioral inhibition and anxiety risk
authors: Filippi, C.A., Massera, A., Xing, J., Martinez Agulleiro, L.
institution: New York University, Department of Child and Adolescent Psychiatry
paradigm: Resting-state and task-evoked EEG; behavioral inhibition
statistical_approach: Review of EEG/ERP studies; frontal asymmetry; multilevel behavioral and neural measures
abstract: This review showcases how studying the neural basis of behavioral inhibition (BI) and maternal anxiety during infancy has advanced understanding of the developmental pathophysiology of anxiety. Infants with BI and those exposed to maternal anxiety/stress exhibit differences in neural processes associated with bottom-up attention and top-down control, both at rest and during stimulus processing. EEG-based markers including frontal asymmetry, neural reactivity, and attentional regulation in infancy are highlighted as promising early biomarkers of anxiety risk.

title: Utilizing functional neuroimaging to study early language development
authors: Olson, H.A., Day, T.K.M., Bradley, H., Manning, B.L., Choi, S., Scheinost, D., Conrad, A.L., McCormick, S.A., Abdurokhmonova, G., Hiersche, K.J.
institution: Northwestern University; University of Iowa; UTHealth Houston; Yale University
paradigm: Review of fetal and infant neuroimaging for language development
statistical_approach: Systematic review; synthesis of fMRI, fNIRS, and EEG language paradigms
abstract: Language develops rapidly over the infant and toddler period and has been a key area of research within developmental cognitive neuroscience. This review takes stock of what functional neuroimaging (fMRI, fNIRS, EEG) has revealed about the neural basis of early language development from the fetal stage through toddlerhood, and explores the biggest open questions for the next phase of research. Understanding the neural mechanisms of early language learning may help predict delays or disorders, recommend early interventions, and provide a deeper mechanistic understanding of how the brain supports language acquisition.

title: Synaptic and intrinsic membrane defects disrupt early neural network dynamics in Down syndrome
authors: Hannan, S.B., Alic, I., Murray, A., Kwon, J., Mortensen, M., Kang, H.J., Plecas, A., Goh, P.A., O'Brien, N.L., Naud, R., Nizetic, D., Smart, T.G.
institution: University College London, Department of Neuroscience, Physiology and Pharmacology
statistical_approach: Isogenic iPSC model; patch-clamp electrophysiology; network burst analysis; pharmacological rescue
abstract: Using an isogenic iPSC-derived neuronal model of trisomy 21, this study shows that Down syndrome impairs cortical circuit activity through deficient glutamatergic synaptic connectivity and aberrant intrinsic membrane properties involving K+ and Na+ channels. Transiently activated A-type K+ channels (Kv4.3) are identified as a key orchestrator during neurodevelopment. These findings link molecular synaptic and membrane phenotypes to emergent network-level disruptions that may underlie early cognitive differences in Down syndrome.

title: Exposome and mental health across the lifespan: research and clinical perspectives
authors: Barzilay, R., Jeste, D.V.
institution: University of Pennsylvania; University of California, San Diego
statistical_approach: N/A (perspective)
abstract: This perspective examines the exposome and its relationship to mental health from childhood through late adulthood. The authors present a framework for integrating exposome research into clinical psychiatry, emphasizing how cumulative environmental burdens interact with biological resilience factors to shape mental health trajectories. The piece calls for exposome-informed approaches to prevention, early identification, and personalized treatment across the lifespan.

title: The exposome and the developing adolescent brain: understanding mechanisms of neuroplasticity
authors: Ho, T.C.
institution: University of California, San Francisco
statistical_approach: N/A (commentary)
abstract: This commentary discusses how the expansion of the exposome during adolescence means that factors influencing experience-dependent mechanisms of neuroplasticity will have an outsized effect on brain development. The piece highlights how environmental contexts during adolescent sensitive periods interact with neurobiological mechanisms of synaptic plasticity, offering a framework for understanding how the exposome shapes brain and mental health trajectories across the lifespan.

title: The effect of the exposome on developmental brain health and cognitive outcomes
authors: Robinson, H., Dave, N., Barzilay, R., et al.
institution: University of Connecticut
statistical_approach: Systematic review; exposome analysis; multivariate environmental risk assessment
abstract: This review addresses how each child's unique constellation of environmental exposures (the exposome) shapes neurodevelopment and cognitive outcomes across childhood. The growing field of exposomics assesses the additive and interactive effects of multimodal environmental features—biological, chemical, physical, and psychosocial—in relation to brain health. The review synthesizes evidence on how prenatal and early postnatal exposures affect developmental trajectories of cognitive function, with implications for identifying critical windows of neuroplasticity and designing preventive interventions.

title: Early dynamics of the genome in Down syndrome
authors: Li, Z.
institution: [UNVERIFIED]
statistical_approach: N/A (perspective)
abstract: This Perspective discusses cutting-edge molecular approaches identifying the earliest genomic changes occurring in the brains of individuals with Down syndrome, establishing how trisomy 21 affects the brain's cells from before birth through 3 years of age. The piece contextualizes recent multiomic studies and their implications for understanding the developmental trajectory of Down syndrome neurobiology, accompanying the Risgaard et al. (2026) empirical report in the same Science issue.

title: Molecular and cellular processes disrupted in the early postnatal Down syndrome prefrontal cortex
authors: Risgaard, R.D., Hanthanan Arachchilage, K., Knaack, S.A., Hosseini, M., Chen, R.J., Kumarage, P., Schmidt, D.K., Huang, X., Sheng, J., Wang, C.J., Giusti, E., Liu, S., Zhang, S.C., Wang, D., Bhattacharyya, A., Sousa, A.M.M.
institution: University of Wisconsin-Madison, Waisman Center
statistical_approach: Single-nucleus RNA-seq; chromatin accessibility profiling; bioinformatic clustering; differential gene expression analysis
abstract: Using single-nucleus multiomic sequencing to simultaneously profile gene expression and chromatin accessibility in the Down syndrome (trisomy 21) prefrontal cortex during early postnatal development, this study reveals widespread dysregulation across metabolic and synaptic pathways, impaired oligodendrocyte lineage progression with deficits in myelin-related transcription, and a pronounced neuroinflammatory signature marked by microglial activation and cytokine dysregulation. These findings establish a molecular foundation linking trisomy 21 to early postnatal disruptions in prefrontal cortical development.

title: Effects of depression genetic risk and household socioeconomic status on emotional behavior and brain development in early adolescence
authors: Campbell, E.M., Morrel, J., Gauderman, W.J., Herting, M.M.
institution: University of Southern California, Los Angeles, CA
statistical_approach: Polygenic risk scores for major depression; ABCD Study; structural and functional MRI; gene × environment interaction models; multivariate analysis linking SES and genetic risk to brain development
abstract: This study examined how polygenic risk for major depression and household socioeconomic status (SES) jointly shape emotional behavior and brain development in early adolescence using the ABCD Study. Polygenic depression risk and low household SES interacted to predict both emotional behavioral outcomes and structural/functional MRI metrics, suggesting that socioeconomic environments moderate genetic vulnerability to depression during a critical period of brain development.

title: Connectome-based predictive modeling of concurrent and longitudinal substance use vulnerability in adolescence
authors: Guassi Moreira, J.F., Allgaier, N., Johnson, S.B., Potter, A.S., Garavan, H., Fair, D.A.
institution: University of Oregon, Eugene, OR; Oregon Health & Science University, Portland, OR
statistical_approach: Connectome-based predictive modeling (CPM); resting-state fMRI; ABCD Study longitudinal data; whole-brain functional connectivity prediction of concurrent and prospective substance use initiation
abstract: Using connectome-based predictive modeling (CPM) applied to resting-state fMRI from the ABCD Study, this study identified functional connectivity profiles predicting concurrent and longitudinal substance use vulnerability in adolescence. Whole-brain connectivity patterns differentiated adolescents at higher versus lower risk, pointing to frontostriatal and frontolimbic circuits as neural markers of adolescent substance use vulnerability.

title: Maternal technoference decreases brain-to-brain synchrony during mother-infant interaction
authors: van den Heuvel, M.I., Mosińska, M., Turk, E., et al.
institution: Tilburg University, Tilburg, Netherlands
statistical_approach: Dual-EEG hyperscanning; inter-brain synchrony (IBS) analysis; mother-infant face-to-face interaction with smartphone technoference condition; linear mixed-effects models
abstract: This dual-EEG hyperscanning study measured brain-to-brain synchrony between mothers and infants during face-to-face interaction, with and without maternal smartphone use (technoference). Maternal smartphone use significantly reduced inter-brain synchrony in mother-infant dyads, linking everyday digital distraction to diminished neural coupling that underpins social learning and emotional development in early infancy.

title: Neural specialization of print processing in second language learning: A longitudinal ERP study of Chinese children learning English
authors: Huang, J., Ariza, J., Huo, J., Lo, J.C.M., McBride, C., Maurer, U.
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong
statistical_approach: Longitudinal ERP design (T1, T2 follow-up); N170/print-specific ERP components; linear mixed-effects models; print tuning index; Chinese children learning English as L2
abstract: This longitudinal ERP study tracked neural print specialization during English second-language (L2) learning in Chinese-speaking children. Results showed progressive tuning of occipitotemporal ERP responses (N170) to English words over the course of L2 learning, indicating that L1 orthographic neural circuits adapt to accommodate L2 print processing, with experience-dependent specialization observed across the study period.

title: Visual Word Form Area demonstrates individual and task-agnostic consistency but inter-individual variability
authors: Mitchell, D., Fuentes-Jimenez, D., Stone, A., Yablonski, M., Yeatman, J.D.
institution: Stanford University, Stanford, CA
statistical_approach: fMRI task-based localizer; individual-level VWFA characterization; split-half reliability analysis; inter-individual variability assessment; reading development cohort
abstract: The visual word form area (VWFA) is central to reading, but its individual consistency versus population variability is debated. This study showed that the VWFA demonstrates high within-individual consistency across tasks and sessions (task-agnostic reliability) yet shows substantial inter-individual variability in location, size, and selectivity. These findings have implications for understanding reading development and dyslexia, underscoring that the VWFA is a consistent individual-level marker but not a fixed population-level region.

title: Neonatal brain MRI to prognosticate neurodevelopmental outcomes in fetal growth restricted infants: a systematic review
authors: Ramdial, K., MacNamara, N., Moldrich, R., Colditz, P., Wixey, J.
institution: University of Queensland, Brisbane, Australia
statistical_approach: Systematic review (PRISMA); neonatal MRI predictors of neurodevelopmental outcomes in fetal growth restriction (FGR); brain volume, cortical maturation, and diffusion MRI; outcome measures: Bayley scales, IQ, motor scores
abstract: This systematic review evaluated whether neonatal brain MRI can reliably predict neurodevelopmental outcomes in infants with fetal growth restriction (FGR). Structural MRI parameters—including brain volume, cortical maturation indices, and white matter diffusion metrics—were assessed across included studies. Results indicate that neonatal MRI abnormalities in FGR infants predict poorer motor, cognitive, and behavioral outcomes, supporting the clinical utility of early MRI prognostication in this high-risk neonatal population.

title: Newborn brain development comparison of plateau and plain regions: insights from quantitative EEG
authors: Zhao, Y., Ze, X., Li, H., et al.
institution: West China Second University Hospital, Sichuan University, Chengdu, China
statistical_approach: Quantitative EEG (qEEG); comparative study of 72 newborns from plateau (>2,500 m altitude) vs. plain regions; spectral power analysis; burst suppression ratio; background EEG maturation indices
abstract: This study compared quantitative EEG features between newborns from high-altitude plateau (>2,500 m) and plain regions to characterize the effects of altitude on early brain development. High-altitude newborns showed altered qEEG spectral patterns compared to plain-region controls, with differences in delta-band power and sleep-state organization, suggesting that chronic hypoxia at high altitude modifies postnatal cortical maturation.

title: Association between neonatal brain volume and school-age executive function in children born moderate-to-late preterm
authors: Rossetti, A.C., Pascoe, L., Mainzer, R., et al.
institution: Murdoch Children's Research Institute / Royal Women's Hospital, Melbourne, Australia
age_range: Neonates at scan (0-12 months), followed to school age (7-9 years)
statistical_approach: Neonatal structural MRI (brain volume, regional morphometry) in moderate-to-late preterm (MLP) infants; longitudinal follow-up to school age; multiple regression; executive function outcomes (CANTAB/BRIEF); mediation analyses
abstract: This longitudinal study examined whether neonatal brain volumes in children born moderate-to-late preterm (32–36 weeks' gestation) predict school-age executive function. Smaller neonatal whole-brain and regional volumes were associated with poorer executive function at school age, providing evidence that early brain growth restriction in MLP preterm infants has lasting cognitive consequences beyond those detectable by standard clinical assessment.

title: Bridging the gap: Translating fetal, infant, and toddler neuroimaging insights into clinical practice
authors: Nelson, D.J., Sacks, D., Korom, M., Remec, N., Perkins, A., Cook, J., Stoyell, S., Evans, D., Moser, J., Capparini, C., Inala, S., Huszar, K., Zieff, G., Vernetti, A., Norton, E.S., Wilkinson, C.L., Arichi, T.
institution: Harvard Medical School / Boston Children's Hospital, Boston, MA; King's College London, London, UK
statistical_approach: Narrative/systematic review; clinical translation framework covering MRI, fNIRS, EEG, and multimodal approaches; barriers and facilitators for NICU and outpatient applications; evidence synthesis across fetal, neonatal, and toddler neuroimaging literature
abstract: This review bridges the gap between developmental neuroimaging research and clinical practice by synthesizing current evidence from fetal, neonatal, infant, and toddler neuroimaging studies. The authors describe how MRI, EEG, and fNIRS findings can inform clinical assessment, monitoring, and early intervention for high-risk infant populations. Key barriers and enabling factors for clinical translation are discussed, with a focus on scalable tools for preterm and neurodivergent infants.

title: Children exhibit greater persistence of motor learning-related patterns of hippocampal activity into post-task wake epochs
authors: Van Roy, A., Temudo, A., Taylor, E.K., Koppelmans, V., Hödlmoser, K., Albouy, G., King, B.R.
institution: KU Leuven, Belgium; University of Utah, Salt Lake City, UT; University of Salzburg, Austria
